data.title: Alphabet's AI Moat: Why The Market Is Undervaluing A Giant
data.description:
Thesis

Alphabet (GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) represents a strong bull case. While the market is highly focused on the regulatory and AI competition headwinds the company is facing, it may be mispricing the giant. This may be due to overlooking the company’s options for monetizing its AI models, the growth of Google Cloud, and the strength of its Search and YouTube businesses. The company currently trades at a price-to-earnings multiple of approximately 20x, which offers a discount compared to both the S&P and its peers. Furthermore, Google’s reputation for lagging in the AI race may not be deserved, due to the company’s success with monetizing its AI features and driving user engagement.

Alphabet Overview

It is easy to view Alphabet as a series of siloed businesses, but this fails to appreciate the true competitive strength that lies in its deeply connected ecosystem. The key to this system is the self-reinforcing flywheel that these segments of the business create.

Google Services

While Alphabet is involved in many different markets and ventures, Google Services remains the engine of the company. This segment includes Search, YouTube, Android, Chrome, Maps, and the Play Store. Not surprisingly, this critical part of the company generates substantial revenue and operating income, with these figures topping $220 billion in 2024. Furthermore, this is a high-margin segment that regularly posts operating margins above 35%.

These services are also the core of Alphabet’s massive user base. The company has 15 products that serve half a billion users. Six of these products, including Search, Android, YouTube, and Gmail, serve over two billion users each. This reach provides Alphabet with unique advantages when it comes to its AI ambitions. For example, the size of Alphabet’s infrastructure makes distributing new features cost less while offering cross-selling opportunities.

Google Cloud

A challenge for companies of Alphabet’s size can be finding new growth opportunities. For Alphabet, Google Cloud has become a critical source of growth. This segment of the business’s operations has achieved an annual revenue run-rate of over $50 billion, and it is growing at an impressive 32% year-over-year rate. Google Cloud has also become sustainably profitable, as it posted $2.8 billion in operating income for the first quarter of 2025.

Alphabet’s Venture Fund

The company's “Other Bets” segment acts as an internal venture capital fund that is strategically used to pursue high-risk but high-reward ventures. This portion of the company is currently generating losses that amount to over $1 billion per quarter. However, these investments represent Alphabet’s entry into developing markets that have high potential. A perfect example of this is the company’s position in Waymo, the autonomous taxi company. Despite being high-risk, these ventures represent tremendous future growth potential for the company that is difficult for markets to effectively price into the company’s value.

The Research and Development Core

The company’s research and development segment is responsible for many critical assets. DeepMind is an example of this, as it is the lab responsible for creating Google’s Gemini models. This division also acts as a crucial hub for research whose costs are absorbed by the company as a whole but whose innovations power many of the other segments of Alphabet.

The Alphabet Flywheel

When evaluating Alphabet, it is beneficial to avoid making the mistake of viewing the various segments of the company as individual parts. In reality, these various segments work to reinforce each other, which offers Alphabet a powerful advantage. More specifically, this flywheel is composed of Alphabet’s data, AI, and distribution infrastructure.

Google Services plays several roles in its strategy. It acts both as a data collection and distribution system for the company’s AI products. This data acts as a valuable source of training data for the AI models, which provides Alphabet with a quantity and quality of data that most AI companies do not possess. These improved models are then integrated into the Google Services ecosystem, which boosts the value of the products under this umbrella. Lastly, the AI products and infrastructure are packaged and licensed to enterprise clients through Google Cloud. The unique interplay of this flywheel is a powerful competitive advantage that can also be easy to underestimate.

Alphabet’s Strengths and Catalysts

Core Business Resilience and AI Success

Concerns have been raised that AI could potentially cannibalize Alphabet’s primary revenue streams. Yet, the company’s recent performance contradicts the cannibalization concerns. Data from Alphabet’s first half of 2025 showcases the resilience of the company’s core businesses along with the effectiveness of its long-term AI monetization strategy. Search and related services showed accelerating strength by posting 12% year-over-year growth during the second quarter. This was an increase from the 10% growth during the first quarter.

In another example of the company’s self-reinforcing flywheel model, its AI Overviews are proving to be highly effective. They are currently used by billions of people a month, and they add to overall search usage. Furthermore, they are monetizing at rates that are similar to traditional search. As a result, the idea of them replacing search behavior is weakening in favor of them adding to user engagement.

Alphabet’s Strategic Cloud Wins

Google Cloud is further solidifying its position as a key enabler of the company’s efforts to capture a share of the enterprise AI market. This segment’s 32% growth in the second quarter is a reminder of this segment’s potential. The number of deals that the company secured with large-scale enterprise clients has surged, as the total value of these deals for the first half of 2025 has already exceeded the total for all of 2024. As an example of the scale of these deals, Alphabet recently secured a $10 billion deal with Meta (META).

Alphabet’s Capex Catalyst

A strong signal of management’s confidence is the recent revision of its capital expenditure forecast, which was increased to $85 billion. This investment is a long-term offensive investment in the company that is partially in response to increasing customer demand.

Much of this will be dedicated to building data centers and other infrastructure to support the substantial technical demands large commercialized AI models require. While these expenditures are substantial, they will help to support the company’s strategy for years as it continues to develop more powerful AI products.

Alphabet’s Financial Engine

Alphabet’s flywheel strategy for competing in the emerging AI market requires immense resources for building data centers, acquiring GPUs, and TPUs, along with competing for world-class talent. To this end, Alphabet’s profitability and strong balance sheets are essential components for supporting the company’s AI strategy.

Cash Flow and Profitability

Alphabet’s AI flywheel has been costly to build, and it still requires constant large investments into research and development through Google DeepMind in order to remain competitive. Fortunately, Alphabet’s core business has remained extremely strong, with it generating over $115 billion in net income during the trailing twelve months. In addition to generating cash, the company is also extremely effective in terms of margin and return on equity. For net income margin, the company has a healthy 31.12% and a return on equity of 34.83%. These healthy numbers show that Alphabet has ample resources for supporting its crucial AI flywheel strategy.

A Fortress Balance Sheet

While Alphabet has found some early success in its AI strategy, the market is still competitive, and the company has yet to meaningfully distance itself from its competitors. This will require the company to sustain its current capital expenditures into AI until it has created a long-term competitive moat. As a result, its fortress balance sheet becomes even more valuable to the company.

This stability rests on the company’s $95.15 billion in total cash and its low Total Debt to Equity ratio of 11.48%. As a result, the company enjoys a comfortable cushion and strategic flexibility without having to significantly limit funding to segments of its flywheel. The ability to sustain investing in its core operations while pursuing the rapidly developing AI market is a critical strength that helps to support a bullish outlook for the company.

A Fundamental Approach to Valuing Alphabet

A discounted cash flow analysis is one way to value Alphabet’s stock, and this analysis shows that the stock may have an intrinsic value of approximately $238. Much of this rests on the premise that Alphabet’s investments in AI will help to drive accelerating growth along with expanding profitability over the company’s long term.

Key Assumptions of the DCF

In order to utilize a DCF model, there are several key assumptions that must be made. In the case of Alphabet, starting with a growth rate of 13% in 2025 and accelerating to 15% by 2027 seems reasonable given the company’s recent performance and the monetization prospects AI offers. Furthermore, this model assumes improving efficiencies and a richer product mix, leading to the EBIT margin increasing from 33.16% to 35.16% by 2027.

CapEx is another important attribute for this model, but this is a slightly complex topic given Alphabet's massive expenditures on AI infrastructure. For the model, an assumption of CapEx normalizing to 9% of revenue is used, which is reasonable given the company’s five-year average of 11.80%.

Weighted Average Cost of Capital is another key value in the model. 8.2% is a reasonable assumption for this variable, as it reflects the likelihood of Alphabet successfully executing its AI strategy. Terminal growth is another key consideration that reflects the amount the company will generate beyond the model’s timeframe. For a company of Alphabet’s size and reach, a terminal growth rate of 4.0% is reasonable, as it is above inflation while still representing the intense competition that Alphabet could face as the AI market continues to mature.

These calculations suggest a bullish future for Alphabet with substantial upside. However, a key assumption of this projection is the revenue growth and expanding margins, which will require the company’s AI strategy to be successful.

Potential Headwinds for Alphabet

Regulatory Challenges

Alphabet’s size and influence across a range of markets make it a common target for regulatory scrutiny. This has reached new heights as the company is currently facing antitrust scrutiny in both the US and Europe. In the US, the legal action is primarily targeting the company’s search monopoly. However, Europe is targeting its ad-tech, which has preliminarily been ruled in non-compliance with the Digital Markets Act. The risks from these legal threats range from a breakup of the company to hefty fines and other punitive actions against the company.

AI Disruption of its Core Business

While Alphabet is investing heavily in AI, it should be noted that AI has the potential to disrupt Alphabet’s key search and advertising businesses. In particular, AI-native search engines could fundamentally alter how users interact with these services. This could put Alphabet on the defensive, requiring it to dramatically change one of its core pillars. An example of this could be the inclusion of AI overviews into search results. These overviews increase user engagement, but they reduce the click-through rate for websites.

Intense Cloud Competition

Google Cloud’s impressive growth makes it an important part of Alphabet’s future. However, it remains third in market share within cloud computing, with Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT) leading. In its efforts to gain market share, Alphabet does benefit from the fact that Google Cloud is growing faster than its rivals, but it requires ongoing investments in order to close this gap. As a result, an execution failure in this segment could pose a risk for Alphabet’s AI plans and growth opportunities.

Conclusion

The evidence strongly indicates that Alphabet’s stock is trading at a discount relative to its intrinsic value. The company’s flywheel structure for developing and distributing its AI products, along with its strong financials, gives it the resources to continue growing. This has been evidenced by the success of Google Cloud and its Gemini models. Together, these factors make Alphabet a buying opportunity with a potential price target of $238.

data.title: Lakeland Financial: Earnings To Continue To Grow But Valuation Is Unattractive
data.description:
Lakeland Financial Corporation’s (LKFN) stock price has surged since I upgraded it to a hold rating in March. The earnings, meanwhile, have grown mostly in line with expectations. For the remainder of the year, I’m expecting earnings to continue to grow because the Fed funds rate cuts will provide an opportunity to lower deposit costs. However, I’m expecting loan growth to slow down because the outlook for Indiana’s agriculture and manufacturing industries, LKFN’s main focus areas, is lackluster. My valuation analysis shows that there isn’t much price upside potential. Additionally, the dividend yield is low. Hence, I’m maintaining a hold rating on Lakeland Financial.

Outlook for Agriculture and Manufacturing Loans is Lackluster

Lakeland Financial’s loan portfolio has grown by 2.5% in the first half of the year, or 5.1% annualized, which is better than the last two years. To determine the outlook for loan growth for the remainder of the year, we need to look at the company’s markets. Lakeland Financial mostly operates in Indiana. As shown below, the company focuses on commercial loans.

Further, the top industries it lends to include Multifamily housing and agriculture.

The top agricultural products of Indiana include corn, soybeans, and meat, according to the Indiana State Department of Agriculture. Corn and soybean prices are now lower compared to last year, which is bad news for farm income and, consequently, the growth of agricultural loans. On the other hand, meat prices are up compared to last year.

The strained relations with major trade partners, Canada and China, could potentially hurt agricultural exports. However, so far, exports have been mostly satisfactory.

Indiana’s economy is highly reliant on manufacturing industries. According to the US Bureau of Labor Statistics, manufacturing jobs make up around 16% of total non-farm jobs in the state. Further, according to the US Bureau of Economic Analysis, manufacturing contributes 28.5% to Indiana’s GDP. Therefore, the Purchasing Managers’ Index (“PMI”) for manufacturing can give us a good idea about the demand for Lakeland Financial’s credit products. Unfortunately, the PMI index continues to be stuck in the contractionary territory, i.e., below 50.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the loan book to grow by 3.2% annualized in the second half of 2025, below the first half’s growth rate of 5.1% annualized. Further, I’m expecting deposits to grow in tandem with loans.

Deposit Re-pricing Likely to Outpace Loan Re-pricing Thereby Lifting the Margin

Lakeland Financial’s net interest margin has grown by an impressive 17 basis points over the first half of this year. The management mentioned in the second quarter’s earnings presentation that deposits have re-priced faster than loans during the current monetary easing cycle, which has led to an improvement in the margin.

I’m expecting the Federal Reserve to cut the Fed funds rate by 25 basis points in September and another 25 basis points in December 2025. These rate cuts will give the management the opportunity to further reduce deposit costs. Interest-bearing demand, savings, and money market accounts made up 67% of total deposits at the end of June, as mentioned in the presentation. Hence, around 67% of deposits can be expected to re-price down soon after the rate cuts.

The benefit of the deposit re-pricing will be countered by the downward re-pricing of loans. As shown below, variable-rate loans made up 54% of total loans.

Keeping the above factors in mind, I’m expecting the net interest margin to grow by 6 basis points in the second half of 2025.

Expecting the EPS to Grow by 9% to $3.97

Lakeland Financial’s earnings for the first half of this year have been mostly in line with my expectations. The company reported an EPS of $1.82, up 2.2% from the corresponding period last year.

For the second half of the year, I’m expecting the earnings to be higher than the first half’s earnings because of continued margin expansion and low loan growth. In addition, I’m making the following assumptions.

  1. I’m expecting the provision expense to remain near the second quarter’s level.
  2. I’m assuming the non-interest income will remain rangebound near the second quarter’s level.
  3. I’m expecting the efficiency ratio to remain near the second quarter’s level.

Overall, I’m estimating an EPS of $3.97 for 2025, up by 9% year-over-year. My new EPS estimate is 7% higher than my previous estimate of $3.70. Please note that I haven’t made any big changes to any income statement line item. I have made small changes in all line items, which have combined to give a material upward revision.

Risks Include Tariffs

The biggest risk to my investment thesis is the tariffs and their impact on inflation. If inflation rises by more than expected, then the Fed might hold back on interest rate cuts, which will take away the opportunity to lower deposit costs.

As per latest news reports, a federal appeals court in Washington, D.C., has ruled that the global tariffs are illegal. Immediately after the ruling, the US President posted on social media that the tariffs were still in effect (according to the same news report linked above). I think the government will push the tariffs through Congress to make them legal, like the spending bill was pushed through in July, as per news reports. Therefore, I'm expecting the tariffs to continue for now.

Maintaining a Hold Rating

I upgraded Lakeland Financial to a hold rating from my previous rating of sell in March 2025. Since then, the stock price has surged by around 12%. Further, the short interest has dropped, although it’s still very high.

To update my valuation, I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $69.6 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $74.7 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $72.1, which suggests a 5.4% upside from the August 29 closing price. Lakeland Financial also offers a low dividend yield of around 2.9%. Adding the price upside to the dividend yield gives a total expected return of 8.1%. Hence, I’m maintaining a hold rating on Lakeland Financial.

The company’s earnings are likely to grow in the year ahead because Fed funds rate cuts will reduce deposit costs and boost the margin. However, loan growth will likely slow down because the agriculture and manufacturing industries in Indiana are facing some headwinds. Overall, I think a hold rating is appropriate for the company because the valuation isn’t attractive enough.

data.title: Permian Resources: Undervalued Permian Pureplay With A Dividend Yield Above 4%
data.description:
Investment thesis

Permian Resources (PR) is the second largest Permian pureplay and trades at an attractive valuation. Given its highest dividend yield among peers and opportunistic share buybacks, I expect the company to regain its previous valuation, giving a conservative fair value of $19 per share, representing a 35% upside.

Background

Permian Resources is an oil and gas company and the second largest Permian pureplay with around 385,000 boe/d. The company resulted from the 2022 merger of Centennial Resource and Colgate Energy, positioning itself as a focused play on the Delaware Basin.

Permian Resources is both in the Delaware and Midland Basins, holding 470,000 net leasehold acres and 100,000 net royalty acres, which provide over 15 years of high quality inventory. Its production mix is approximately 45% oil, with gas being a significant component. For example, in Q2, of the 385k boe/d, 176k boe/d was oil, 98k boe/d was NGLs, and 111k boe/d was gas. However, with all the liquefaction capacity coming online, I expect natural gas prices to have more upside potential than oil prices.

The company has learned from previous periods, and it is focused on creating long-term value, with the main objective of growing FCF per share.

The management team is aligned since they own more than 6% of the company, which also favors strong shareholder returns. Last year the company updated its shareholders’ return plan, establishing a base $0.15 quarterly dividend and a $1 billion repurchase program. The idea was to give predictability to the market and eliminate the supplemental dividend; moreover, the objective is to increase the base dividend over time with production increases. The current dividend is sustainable until a $40 WTI price. The repurchase program is opportunistic, and the company will use it when it considers there is a dislocation in the stock price. For example, they used it during the April selloff, repurchasing 4.1 million shares at an average price of $10.52, almost the bottom.

The company is also focused on increasing production, following two different approaches. Investing in its own acreage and accretive M&A, since 2024 the company has invested $2 billion in acquisitions. In Q2, the company closed the acquisition of APA Corp (APA) assets in the Delaware basin. Such transaction allowed the company to increase the production guidance while reducing the CapEx.

Finally, the company has entered into multiple transportation and marketing agreements to improve the realized prices. The company expects to increase its FCF by $50 million due to these new agreements.

Financial Position and Valuation

Permian Resources has a strong financial position with $451 million in cash and $4 billion in debt. The company has some near term maturities; however, most of its debt matures after 2030.

It is one of the lowest leveraged fracking companies with a 1x net debt to EBITDAX, which is expected to come down to 0.8x by year end.

This has allowed it to achieve the investment grade classification by Fitch, with S&P and Moody’s expected in the near future.

At the same time, it is one of the cheapest stocks while paying one of the highest (yet sustainable) dividend.

In fact, the company was very well correlated with FANG’s valuation, its closest peer, until last year:

I expect that, with the clear dividend policy and increased guidance, the company could trade more in line with its historical valuation and approach FANG’s valuation, as it did in the past.

Assuming a 6x EV/EBITDA and annualizing the Q2 EBITDA, the stock price should trade at $21 per share.

Given the risks that I will review in the next section, I believe that the fair value should be slightly lower due to possible declines in the oil price. My fair value is $19 per share, which implies a yield slightly above 3% and still one of the highest among peers.

Risks

As an oil and gas company, the main risk for Permian Resources is the commodity prices. In Q2, the company already experienced a pronounced decline in realized prices, from $70.48 per barrel of oil, $1.35 per Mcf of natural gas, and $23.90 per barrel of NGL in Q1 to $62.71 per barrel of oil, $17.75 per barrel of NGL and $0.53 per Mcf of natural gas in Q2. This decline reduced EBITDAX from $1 billion in Q1 to $890 million in Q2.

I don’t expect gas prices to decline further, but especially for oil, there could be some pressure. Assuming WTI prices are around $55, the company’s EBITDAX will slide to around $800 million per quarter. With that EBITDAX, a 6x multiple will imply a $18 share price; however, in that scenario the multiple will probably be lower. In that case, a 5x multiple justifies a $14.2 share price, which is in line with the current share price.

Related to this, the company has only hedged about 32% of its H2-2025 production, which I consider very low, given that in Q2 they added hedges at $70 per barrel. I believe that they should have hedged more volumes, both for the second half of 2025 and for 2026.

Another risk is overpaying for acquisitions. If acquisitions are expensive or if oil prices decline, it could make some acquisitions unprofitable, and the company will have increased its debt without a good return.

Conclusion

Permian Resources’ valuation is appealing at current WTI prices while it offers one of the highest dividends in the industry. Given the sustainable dividend and opportunistic share repurchases, I expect that the company will regain its previous valuation and trade more in line with FANG, as it did in the past.

However, if oil prices slide further, the company could already be fully valued. In that case, I expect the share price to decline, and I will start a position.

data.title: Accelerating Growth In AI And Cloud Makes Tencent Still A Buy
data.description:
In mid-May 2025, I published my last article about Tencent Holdings Limited (TCEHY)(OTCPK:TCTZF), and rated the stock as a “Buy” – once again. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

In my opinion, Tencent is still a “Buy” and the stock still seems to be undervalued and might be trading about 20% below its intrinsic value. Of course, the stock is not a screaming “Buy” and it might not be the best time to purchase the stock anymore. Additionally, the level of uncertainty is extremely high, and we don’t know whether geopolitics might have a huge negative impact on the economy (and Tencent as a business). Nevertheless, I think Tencent is one of the best businesses in the market, and I am quite confident that the stock will trade higher in 5 and 10 years from now.

During the last 3.5 months, Tencent increased about 18% in value and therefore the stock not only had a great annualized return but also outperformed the S&P 500. In the following article, I will argue once again that the Chinese technology company is still a “Buy” – especially following the latest quarterly results, which were without doubt great results once again.

Quarterly Results

On August 13, 2025, the company reported second quarter results for fiscal 2025 and not only did Tencent beat revenue expectations, but also expectations for earnings per share. Looking at growth rates year-over-year, revenue increased from RMB 161,117 million in Q2 '24 to RMB 184,504 million in Q2 '25 – resulting in 14.5% top line growth. Operating profit increased 18.5% year-over-year from RMB 50,732 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 60,104 million in this quarter, and operating margin also improved from 36.3% to 37.5% in the same timeframe. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased even 20.0% year-over-year from RMB 4.994 in Q2 '24 to RMB 5.996 in Q2 '25.

Looking at the results in more detail, we see all three segments contributing to growth. FinTech and Business Services, which was in the last few years one of the major drivers of growth, grew “only” 10.1% year-over-year to RMB 55,536 million. Value Added Services grew its revenue 15.9% year-over-year to RMB 91,368 million. The biggest driver of growth was Marketing Services, which increased its revenue 19.7% year-over-year to RMB 35,762 million.

AI Accelerating Growth

Tencent was not necessarily one of the major names among the huge technology companies in the last two or three years when we talked mostly about LLM models. In this regard, OpenAI, Alphabet or Baidu were the companies mentioned most often. Although Tencent might not have the best-performing LLM, it seems like the company knows how to generate additional revenue by using AI and growing its business.

I already mentioned above that Marketing Services reported the highest growth rate among the three different segments, and AI played a crucial part in achieving these high growth rates. During the last earnings call, Chief Strategy Officer James Gordon Mitchell commented:

For Marketing Services, revenue grew 20% year-on-year to RMB 36 billion in the quarter, benefiting from AI-powered adtech upgrades and from increased closed-loop advertising arising from Weixin's transactional ecosystem. We expanded AI capabilities in areas, including ad creation, placement, recommendation and performance analysis, which had the effect of boosting click-through rates, conversions and ROI for advertisers.

In 2023, Tencent added LLM capabilities for automation and acceleration of ad creative production and faster and more accurate approval of ad content. During the first half of 2025, the foundation model was scaled up to analyze user interactions with text, image and video and to determine user interests.

Especially Video Accounts marketing services, Mini programs marketing services and Weixing search marketing services are playing a crucial role for Tencent right now as all three grow revenue about 50% to 60% year-over-year. And when being asked about the growth potential of the advertising business, management was quite optimistic about the long-term growth and outlined the different levers they can pull in the years to come:

So on the advertising and the potential, we continue to believe that we enjoy a long and lengthening runway for continuing to grow our advertising revenue at a reasonably healthy rate. And that length of the runway reflects upside in a number of the key variables that determine our marketing services revenue, including the click-through rate where AI delivers better targeting and thus more clicks, including traffic where we see growth in video accounts traffic and search traffic over time in traffic within our AI native experiences, including revenue per click as generative AI used for creating the ads results in more ad demand as well as e- commerce closed-loop transactions resulting in more ad demand.

And then finally, in ad load, where, as you know, for short video, our ad load is currently in the low to mid-single digits versus our peers who are in the low to mid-teens. So those are the reasons we believe there's a long runway for growth for our advertising revenue. In terms of prioritizing between the drivers, then in the second quarter, the majority of the advertising revenue growth of 20% year-on-year arose from higher revenue per impression. And that, in turn, was primarily due to a higher click-through rate arising from deploying AI, although also to higher revenue per click arising from more closed-loop activity with mini shops and mini games.“

Aside from advertising, Tencent is also seeing artificial intelligence boosting its gaming revenue. While “AI” was mentioned 72 times during the earnings call, the term “game” was mentioned even 111 times. During the last earnings call, management stated:

Reviewing the progress of our game business domestically and internationally in recent months, AI has become an increasingly important driver of its growth in terms of game content, game engagement and game monetization. We're increasingly applying AI tools to boost the speed and scale of content production across our major games.

And we see that revenue growth from games has constantly accelerated in the last few quarters since Q4 '23.

And although patents are not automatically leading to revenue or free cash flow, we should not ignore the fact that Tencent is holding the highest number of generative AI patents among all companies in the world.

Becoming a Serious Cloud Competitor

When looking at market shares in Mainland China, Tencent is only the third biggest cloud provider – after Alibaba (BABA) and Huawei with a market share around 10%.

But it seems like Tencent is more and more getting a serious competitor with cloud services revenue growing in the teens in Q2 '25 and Tencent Cloud increasing its international revenue significantly – especially due to the launch of a new cloud region in Japan (the third availability zone in the country). And according to management, there is a clear reason why cloud revenue is growing:

Cloud services revenue growth accelerated versus recent quarters, benefiting from increased revenue from providing GPUs and API tokens for customers' AI needs.

And when asked about the cloud business during the earnings call, the Chief Strategy Officer explained how Tencent restructured its cloud business, which seems to be paying off:

With our business services revenue growth, we did see more revenue related to GPI (sic) [ GPU ] and API token rental. But we also saw broad-based growth. We feel that in the last 2 to 3 years, we've effectively debugged our cloud business by scaling back and sometimes exiting from low margin and low value-add activities. And now that we've put our cloud business onto a more sustainable base as well as improve the cost competitiveness of the supply chain for our cloud business, we can -- we are refocusing on growing revenue at an accelerated rate versus the prior rate without depending too much on the vagaries of the GPU supply situation.

So if we do have sufficient GPUs that we can rent out more in the cloud, then we'll do so. But our cloud strategy is not dependent on the GPUs. We're also growing in CPU, in storage, in database, in CDN and so forth. So that's on the cloud side.

IDC is expecting high growth for the cloud business in the Asia-Pacific region with growth rates of 22% annually for the next few years. And it seems like Tencent is well positioned to profit from this expected growth in the years to come.

Broad-based Growth

With advertising revenue growing, Cloud Business improving and expanding internationally, Games revenue growing at a high pace (especially international games), it seems like Tencent is in a great position and growth is broad-based, although growth rates between the different segments and sub-segments are fluctuating, all three segments are reporting revenue growth in the double-digits. This is without doubt a huge advantage for Tencent: If one business segment will struggle (and this will happen again), the other segments are able to set off the weakness in parts of the business.

It is struggling a little bit in FinTech services, but revenue growth also increased to a high-single digit percentage driven by commercial payment services and consumer loan services. And of course, the number of fee-based VAS paying users increased only slightly (0.4% year-over-year) from 263 million to 264 million users, which is not great. Music subscribers increased 6% to 124 million (with Music subscription revenue increased 17% year-over-year), while Video subscribers declined 3% year-over-year. On the other hand, the combined MAU of Weixin and WeChat increased 2.9% year-over-year to 1,411 million at the end of Q2 '25.

But aside from a few metrics which could be better, we see Tencent performing extremely well and growth being broad-based also becomes obvious when looking at the gross margin. Not only is the gross margin overall improving, all three business segments reporting growing margins over the last two or three years.

And Tencent is not only growing earnings per share and revenue at a high pace, the company is also accelerating revenue growth, and Q2 '25 was the fifth consecutive quarter with higher (or at least constant) year-over-year growth rates.

Like I said, not every single metric is great, but Tencent is coming very close to firing on all cylinders. Right now, Tencent certainly has a resilient business model with diversified revenue streams, all of which are currently contributing to growth.

Analysts are also quite optimistic that Tencent will continue to grow with a high pace. Not only are they expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 13.8% in the next five years (between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2029), but estimates for revenue as well as earnings per share are increased in the last few quarters, which is a good sign for accelerating growth.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

When looking at the simple valuation multiples, we can’t argue that Tencent is a major bargain. But considering a business that is accelerating revenue growth and improving margins, we certainly can’t argue that the current valuation multiples are expensive. Right now, Tencent is trading for 24.4 times earnings and while that valuation multiple seems a bit high, the stock is trading for only 18.2 times free cash flow. And the P/FCF ratio is the more important metric. However, both metrics, the P/E ratio and P/FCF ratio are below the 10-year average and indicate that Tencent is still not expensive at this point.

But as always, valuation multiples are only a first hint whether a stock is overvalued or not. Instead, we calculate an intrinsic value by using a discount cash flow calculation. In my last article I calculated an intrinsic value of HKD 673 for Tencent and the stock would therefore still trade more than 10% below its intrinsic value. Nevertheless, let’s update the intrinsic value calculation a little bit. We still calculate with a 10% discount rate and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding, which is 9,247 million. As basis for our calculation, we once again use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was RMB 153.1 billion.

In my last article, I calculated with 12% growth for the next ten years followed by 6% growth till perpetuity. And in my opinion, we can use these assumptions again, and this leads to an intrinsic value of RMB 633 and HKD 689 (as the stock is trading primarily in this currency). This makes Tencent still about 14% undervalued at this point.

Conclusion

The times in which Tencent must be seen as a major bargain are certainly over. Since its lows in late 2022, the stock price almost tripled from about HKD 200 to HKD 600. Nevertheless, I don’t see Tencent as expensive or overvalued as the stock is still trading below its intrinsic value and below its previous all-time high of around HKD 770, which might serve as a potential price target in the next few quarters for the stock.

Tencent clearly remains a “Buy” and although we should not expect the stock to triple again over the next two or three years, expecting a return on investment of at least 10% for Tencent seems more than reasonable. Of course, this does not mean we should preclude setbacks and corrections in the years to come. Tencent’s stock price might certainly correct for several months before the stock can move higher.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: If You Like LandBridge, You May Love WaterBridge
data.description:
Introduction

This is an article I didn't expect to write this year.

As weird as that may sound, it's because of some unexpected developments, including the just-announced IPO from WaterBridge Infrastructure (WBI), a company that is important because of at least two major reasons:

  • WaterBridge operates at the epicenter of one of my favorite investment themes, which is the booming water business in the Permian Basin. I discussed this in many articles, including one that had the title "Water You Waiting For? One Of My Favorite Ideas I've Ever Written About."
  • WaterBridge shares a management team with LandBridge (NYSE:LB), which is my largest holding and a company that was initially founded to own the land that WaterBridge needs to operate its business. These companies have the same financial sponsor (Five Point Infrastructure) and are part of a large plan to create long-term shareholder value in American energy.

Because of these developments, I get to discuss both companies. I will tell you what this WaterBridge IPO is all about, where I see value, and how this impacts LandBridge, which I discussed on August 8 using the title "I Have Never Been More Bullish On LandBridge."

So, let's keep this intro short and get right to it, as we have a lot to discuss!

WBI, Its Business Model, And Ownership

Roughly 14 months ago, I was the first to cover LandBridge, a few days after it went public. Despite losing roughly 40% of its market cap since its all-time high, LandBridge shares are still up more than 110% since their debut, as I believe the company went public when "nobody" really understood what the company was about.

Initially, it was seen as a mini version of Texas Pacific Land (TPL), which is the biggest public landowner in the Permian Basin that generates roughly half of its revenue from oil and gas royalties.

While it's fair to call LandBridge a mini-Texas Pacific Land, it's more than that, as LandBridge generates less than 10% of its revenue from oil and gas royalties. The overwhelming majority of its income comes from operations like surface use royalties and resource sales. This includes pipeline easements and a royalty on every barrel of freshwater and produced water on its land.

Unlike Texas Pacific Land, which was founded as a result of the Texas and Pacific Railroad bankruptcy in 1888, LandBridge was founded with the very specific purpose to benefit from commercial activities in the Permian Basin, which has become the largest oil basin in the United States.

This is what it says in LandBridge's (LB) 2024 10-K (emphasis added):

Five Point and our management team formed LandBridge to acquire, manage and expand a strategic land position in the heart of the Delaware Basin to support the development of WaterBridge’s large-scale produced water handling infrastructure and to actively manage our land and resources to support and encourage broader industrial and commercial development. Since our formation, our management and Five Point have successfully started and expanded businesses that generate new and growing sources of revenue for us by capturing and monetizing commercial activity both on and near our land. - LB 2024 10-K

WaterBridge is one of the companies that benefits from a booming water industry. One of its peers is Aris Water Solutions (ARIS), which I started covering this year. This month, it was bought by Western Midstream (WES) in a move that I expect will yield big results for the public pipeline owner.

However, I hated to see it go, as I would have loved to own Aris at some point.

Now, that gap will be filled by WaterBridge Infrastructure, which just released its S-1 form. If you're new to IPOs, an S-1 form is a mandatory filing with the SEC that informs potential investors about its business model, financial situation, management team, risk factors, and reasons for going public.

In other words, it's a sales pitch with super strict guidelines.

The beauty of WaterBridge's filing is that even if you have no plan to ever invest in this company, you will still get a lot of valuable info on its industry. Especially for LandBridge investors, this is a huge benefit.

Having said all of this, WaterBridge is one of the largest pure-play water infrastructure providers that manages produced water, a byproduct of the oil and gas production process. To use the company's own words:

We operate the largest produced water infrastructure network in the United States through which we provide water management solutions to oil and natural gas exploration and production (“E&P”) companies under long-term contracts, which include gathering, transporting, recycling and handling produced water. - WBI S-1

As of July 31, the company owns 2,500 miles of pipelines, 196 produced water handling facilities, and the capability of handling more than 2.6 million barrels per day, with the capacity to boost this to 4.5 million barrels. Of these assets, more than 1,600 pipeline miles are located in the Delaware Basin, which is a sub-basin of the Permian Basin and the operating area of LandBridge.

The handy map below shows the Delaware Basin, including the company's water pipelines, water handling facilities, announced projects (LandBridge investors will be familiar with the Speedway project), and LandBridge land.

I also need to mention that WaterBridge (and LandBridge) operates in what I consider the best place for long-term production growth, as this part of the Permian Basin has the best oil reserves that will attract much more demand.

Among its customers are some of the largest onshore producers in the United States, most of which I have covered or even traded. As we can see below, its five biggest customers make up about 51% of its water-related income. This includes Permian Resources (PR) and Devon Energy (DVN), which contribute a third of its total revenue.

Most of these contracts (77% to be precise) are long-term deals with a current weighted average remaining term of 11 years. These deals come with acreage dedications, minimum volume commitments, and inflation protection.

In order to support its operations, the company uses its WAVE platform to forecast water volumes, monitor flows in real time, and share data. This platform is important, as WaterBridge operates a highly complex network that supports everything related to oil and gas production, including water supply, produced water takeaway, cleaning water, and much more (see below).

This makes it a super "mission-critical" company in the mighty Permian.

Now, before I tell you more about its growth potential, you may be asking why the company wants to become public. Isn't it enough if LandBridge is a public company? Well, apparently not, as WaterBridge has big plans.

The company, which has not released a specific share count or price range, is working with JPMorgan, Barclays, Goldman Sachs, and Morgan Stanley as underwriters. A total of 13 players are involved.

Current owners are investment units WBR Holdings, NDB Holdings, Desert Holdings, and Devon Energy. Especially Devon has been very active in securing long-term deals as well, as announced by LandBridge a few weeks ago.

Devon Energy has been a huge part of WaterBridge's growth. As a result of a 2023 deal, Devon Energy entered into a long-term agreement that allowed WaterBridge to handle almost all of its produced water in the Delaware region. Devon also contributed 18 produced water handling facilities and pipelines.

Currently, Devon is a co-owner of WaterBridge, along with NDB Holdings (controlled by Five Point), Desert Environmental, and other entities that are essentially owned by Five Point Infrastructure. Five Point and Devon will remain majority shareholders after the IPO. While it is not known just how many shares will be issued to the public, Five Point will use a Class A and Class B structure, as it did with LandBridge. While Class B shares won't have economic rights (they won't receive dividends), they have as much voting power as Class A shares, which means Five Point will remain in charge.

The Five Point Members will have the collective right to designate more than a majority of the members of our board of directors as long as they and their affiliates beneficially own at least 40% of our outstanding common shares. - WBI S-1

To make matters more complicated, the company has an Up-C structure, which means the majority owners, Five Point, Devon, and Elda River, hold economic interest in OpCo, which is taxed as a partnership and comes with a tax-friendly pass-through model. Public shareholders will hold Class A shares in WaterBridge, which is taxed as a normal C-Corp (it won't issue a K-1 form!).

While I do not like it when businesses are managed by a small group of owners, it makes sense in this case, as Five Point has a proven model of establishing value-adding businesses. It has a large portfolio that includes others like Desert Environmental and PowerBridge, the latter works with power providers and data center builders to bring commercial business to Texas.

The reason why the company is going public is to buy back OpCo interests from Elda River and repay debt. The company has not made clear how much debt it will reduce or what its ownership will be after the IPO. This is simply because it is unknown how many shares will be sold.

When it comes to these financial numbers, the S-1 truly looks like a slice of Swiss cheese, as it simply left out many financial numbers.

It has also made no formal dividend announcement. LandBridge didn't do that either. Usually, companies don't announce dividend plans before going public. What we do know is that LandBridge started paying a dividend in the first year of being a public enterprise.

There's A Lot Of Growth To Be Captured

Produced water is a huge business. As I have written in prior articles, it's one of the biggest bottleneck problems in the world. While that may sound like I'm exaggerating, we need to be aware that the Permian Basin was the most dominant driver of oil supply growth since the Great Financial Crisis (thanks to shale). It provided super cheap energy for the U.S. and the world.

Now, this basin struggles with water issues, as in most areas, every barrel of oil comes with at least ten barrels of topix (produced) water. This water needs to be taken care of, as it is a serious problem for oil and gas companies.

This year, roughly 75% of all companies surveyed by the Dallas Fed noted that water was becoming a problem in the drilling process.

Since 2015, the costs to dispose of a barrel of produced water in the Permian have increased from $0.30 to $1.20. In 2006, it was almost "free."

In the Delaware Basin, which has much more oil reserves than the Midland Basin, the other sub-basin of the Permian, water volumes have risen by 21% per year in the 2014-2024 period. To put this into perspective, the Delaware Basin now produces 13.2 million barrels of toxic water per day. That's up from 1.6 million daily barrels in 2014. This number outpaced oil production by nearly 9.0 million barrels per day, as produced water is "booming."

On top of deteriorating oil reserves, which means the water-to-oil ratio becomes more unfavorable, the company sees challenges/opportunities tied to how major producers operate:

The challenges of developing a single-producer network have grown as E&P companies have shifted to larger pad developments. [...] resulting in greater volumes of water concentrated within a given surface location - WBI S-1

As we can see below, Permian produced water volumes are expected to rise consistently in the years ahead, even if oil output is pressured. By the early 2030s, we could even see a scenario of water handling shortages. Although I do not expect this to happen due to new investments, it shows demand is booming and pricing power is strong. On a side note, that is also why I believe Devon Energy closed the water handling deal with LandBridge. Because it starts in 2027, it's likely a move by Devon to get ahead of the surge in pricing.

This has multiple implications:

  • Oil producers will have to spend more on water handling.
  • New Mexico (part of the Delaware Basin) will have to make water disposal operations more favorable. As we can see below, Texas has gotten a lot more disposal demand, as New Mexico's regulations are less favorable. This has caused a lot of water to cross the border to Texas, pressuring pore spaces, as producers have been pumping loads of water back into the ground. That's also why LandBridge's land is so critical.

Because of the decline in available pore space, a much bigger emphasis will be put on recycling produced water. Based on B3 Insight numbers, shared by WaterBridge, we can assume total Delaware Basin operational capacity will drop by 36% by 2035, requiring 9 million barrels of incremental produced water handling capacity.

The chart above is my favorite chart of this article, as it shows just how important the water business is becoming. That's also why the map below is my favorite map of this article, as it shows that produced water volumes from New Mexico will almost all be pushed over LandBridge land, making LB super important for infrastructure construction and pore space availability.

Even better, because cleaning water will be increasingly feasible, surface land can be used for value-added projects, including using water for agricultural and industrial purposes. This includes data center cooling and alfalfa farming. Alfalfa is a crop that is in high demand to feed cattle. Texas Pacific Land has been experimenting with the idea of using cleaned water for programs that further improve the surface value of otherwise barren land.

It would benefit WaterBridge due to water sales and fees tied to the cleaning process. LandBridge would benefit from additional water royalties and surface income.

What About Its Financials?

A big part of the IPO will be used to reduce debt, as I already mentioned. After the IPO, the operating company plans a new $500 million revolving credit facility to replace the existing one and $750 million in senior secured debt to refinance term loans. This aims to lower interest costs, which currently have a weighted average rate of 8.2%, which is very high.

The good news is that net debt was at 4.1x adjusted EBITDA. While it's elevated, it does not suggest this company is in any kind of financial trouble. This number is based on $1.68 billion in long-term debt and $164,000 in cash.

With that said, there's some bad news. For starters, we see that the company reported a net loss of $15.6 million in the quarter ending June 30 (see below).

It generated $86 million in revenues from produced water handling, water solutions, and other operations. This is a 1% increase compared to the prior-year quarter, which is good news in light of challenging oil prices.

However, total operating costs were $64 million, with roughly half of these costs coming from depreciation. That's why the main focus is on adjusted EBITDA, which excludes depreciation. Year-to-date, WBI maintained a 51% adjusted EBTDA ratio, which is very healthy. Aris Water Solutions had a lower margin of 45% during the same period.

Moreover, the company spent $26 million on interest expenses, which is 30% of its entire revenue. This pushed net income to a loss of $15.6 million.

Essentially, this is the result of aggressive growth, which required investments in CapEx. Given that WaterBridge is a young company, these developments are quite normal. If we combine the fact that it saw solid revenues in a very challenging oil environment, the fact that depreciation is a non-cash item, and that interest expenses will decline over time, it makes me believe that this company will be just fine. It just may be a tougher start than LandBridge, as the landowner has no CapEx requirements, a leverage ratio in the low-2.0x range, and very limited operating risks.

Also, note that half of the general and administrative expenses included share-based compensation, which soared by $5.1 million compared to 1H24.

Now What?

While it is too early to make a final decision due to the absence of some numbers, like an initial IPO price range, which is important for the valuation, we have enough data to reach a verdict for the time being.

As a LandBridge (and Texas Pacific Land) shareholder, I have to say that WaterBridge's data gave me a lot of confidence in my thesis. The company confirmed what I had expected, which is that long-term water handling demand is extremely strong, fueled by increasing water-to-oil ratios, looming water handling shortages, and strong demand from operators.

Both WaterBridge and LandBridge are "mission-critical" players, which makes them essential in America's largest oil basin. They are also critical in bringing commercial players to the Permian, as WaterBridge has the hardware and partnerships to clean water and offer value-adding surface services.

With that said, while it is very hard to make a call without knowing what the initial valuation of WaterBridge will be, I can say that I do not feel compelled to buy WaterBridge. Although I believe it is a fantastic business, I think LandBridge covers all bases. I own an ultra-high-margin landowner with a great balance sheet and strong ties to WaterBridge.

If WaterBridge wins, LandBridge wins as well, as royalties and other fees related to operations and volumes on its land will be paid.

That said, I will continue to cover WaterBridge, as it's critical to understand this company, given the importance of the water thesis to my portfolio and because I do not rule out eventually buying the stock if the price is right.

I liked Aris Water Solutions and believe owning something similar makes sense. I just need to get a really good price to justify adding a CapEx-intensive peer to my LandBridge investment.

Once WaterBridge has gotten a handle on its balance sheet, I think the WBI and LB tickers make one of the best energy combos in all of North America.

Stay tuned!

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is a prolonged environment of subdued oil prices. LB investors are currently figuring this out the hard way. The longer oil prices remain close to $60 or lower, the market will increasingly believe growth in the Permian will be limited. That's why I often say that once oil recovers, the market will play catch-up, as it will focus again on the massive opportunities in the water business.

data.title: Tompkins Financial Corporation: Balance Sheet Repricing Remains An Ongoing Tailwind
data.description:
Like many other banks, shares of northeastern lender Tompkins Financial Corporation (TMP) have rallied strongly over the past few months, more than recovering the losses they sustained during the "Liberation Day" panic. As a result, Tompkins has returned over 20% since my opening piece in early April - a performance that has lifted the stock back into positive territory for the year.

As expected, Tompkins has seen a margin-driven uptick in its earnings this year, offsetting modestly higher credit charges. Given the composition of its balance sheet, and particularly its low-yielding securities portfolio, I expect this to be a multi-year process.

As for the stock, after rallying strongly over the past four months, it has re-rated somewhat in terms of its valuation. However, these shares remain at a discount looking at past-average multiples of book value and the dividend yield. With the bank's earnings also set to grow strongly, I remain bullish.

Favorable Repricing Dynamics

Like other banks, balance sheet repricing dynamics have been favorable for Tompkins in recent quarters, and this is currently powering decent earnings growth for the bank. On the funding side, Tompkins already scored very well to begin with. At its peak quarterly rate last year, Tompkins was only paying a blended rate of 2.01% on its funding sources, much lower than the average bank. Of course, the flip side to having cheap funding is that it somewhat limits how much can be gained when interest rates fall. In addition, Tompkins sports a relatively high loan-to-deposit ratio (it was around 92% last quarter), so it can't get too aggressive in allowing its deposits to run off.

Despite this, Tompkins has still seen a modest tailwind from lower rates. It reported a circa 2.44% cost of interest-bearing liabilities last quarter, which was down around 20bps on the year-ago quarter. On top of that, balances of non-interest-bearing deposits have been fairly resilient. They totaled around $1.8 billion on a period-average basis in Q2, which was basically flat year-on-year and equal to a solid 27% of its total deposits.

I have been more interested in the asset side of the ledger. When interest rates rose, Tompkins only saw a modest increase in its own interest-earning asset yield. Indeed, while the effective Fed funds rate went on to rise by around 525bps between Q4 2021 and Q2 2024, Tompkins' earning-asset yield only increased by 140bps, equal to a weak 27% pass-through rate.

This continues to provide an ongoing source of upside, as fixed-rate cash flows that were put on when interest rates were low can be repriced into the higher prevailing environment. Tompkins' securities portfolio provides a good example of this. The bank has held the balance of its these assets relatively flat this year at around the $1.7 billion level. As low-yielding maturities have been reinvested at higher rates, the blended portfolio yield has risen by around 16bps since Q4 2024, clocking in at 2.51% last quarter. Since this remains well below prevailing yields, this repricing process should be an ongoing tailwind for the bank.

Something similar can be seen in the loan portfolio. Tompkins added an extra $200 million to its loan book between Q4 2024 and Q2 2025. As yields on new loans are higher than the average book rate, the latter has increased 9bps in that time, clocking in at 5.40% in Q2. Of course, it has also helped that the Fed has not cut interest rates this year, as the bank's floating-rate loans would reprice relatively quickly to any changes.

Weighing all of that up, these dynamics saw Tompkins' net interest margin ('NIM') expand by 35bps year-on-year last quarter, clocking in at 3.08%. With its interest-earning asset balances also rising by around 4% to $7.9 billion, the bank has seen strong growth in both revenue and earnings. The latter rose 14% year-on-year last quarter to $82.6 million, while pre-provision operating income jumped 36% to roughly $31 million.

That has allowed Tompkins to comfortably absorb an uptick in credit charges. Over the first two quarters, the bank booked $8.1 million in provisioning charges, up from roughly $3 million in the year-before period. Despite this, H1 2025 earnings per share grew around 25% year-on-year to $2.87, while Q2 EPS ($1.50) was up around 35% year-on-year.

Turning back to credit quality for a moment, Tompkins has seen a modest rise in loan losses this year, which partly explains its higher provisioning charge. Net charge-offs totaled around $6 million over the first two quarters, equal to an annualized rate of around 0.2% of total loans. This is still a modest level of losses, and besides, Tompkins has an excellent historical record of limiting net charge-offs, as shown below.

Tompkins' capital position is also fine. The bank reported a healthy 12.1% Common Equity Tier 1 ratio last quarter, mapping to a circa $325 million surplus above its effective 7% minimum requirement. This metric doesn't include the unrealized losses in its securities portfolio, although a circa 8% ratio of tangible common equity to tangible assets isn't much below average. As such, I am not particularly worried about the bank's safety.

The Valuation

Tompkins trades for $70.24 as I type, having risen around 24% since I first covered it. Of course, this also means the stock is now slightly more expensive than it was. Relative to tangible value ($46.31 per share), Tompkins' multiple has expanded by around 0.2x to 1.52x, while the current dividend yield (3.53%) is around 90bps lower than it was back in April.

As you can see above, both metrics actually remain cheap compared to their 10-year averages. Furthermore, Tompkins still has plenty of room for more margin-fueled earnings growth. At 3.08%, last quarter's NIM is still around 30bps below its 2015-2020 average.

As the bank's assets continue to reprice to a higher yield environment, earnings growth should follow. Analysts currently expect Tompkins to earn $6.76 per share next year. Not only is that nearly 20% higher than the H1 2025 run rate ($5.74 per share), but 2026 consensus EPS has also improved by around $0.50 from my opening piece.

This would also put the 1-year forward P/E at a very reasonable 10.4x. As you can see above, Tompkins was trading for ~13x trailing-twelve-month earnings a few years ago. If it earns $6.76 next year, then a similar multiple would put the stock in the high-$80s area by 2027. That would rise into the $90 area once you include the dividend, implying an attractive double-digit annualized total return. For that reason, I remain bullish. I am leaving my 'buy' rating for Tompkins Financial Corporation in place.

data.title: 2 Blue-Chip Behemoths For Retirement Income
data.description:
Investing for retirement income is still possible without having to lock in insignificant dividends or coupons. The conditions are indeed no longer as attractive as right when the Fed started to aggressively hike the interest rates, but the overall situation is at least not that bad.

We all remember how aggressively the capital allocation strategy had to be structured before the COVID-19 hit just to access some cash flows that could move the needle. Back then, investing in ~5% yielding stock was already deemed quite risky with probably weak underlying fundamentals.

In late 2022, we could easily scope 7% to 9% yields that would fit most retirement income strategies (e.g., cash flow stability, no speculation, inflation indexation). The midstream (AMLP) and REIT (VNQ) segments are the immediate examples that come into my mind.

Now, however, we are talking about 5% to 7% yield range in which the risks remain balanced and appropriate for prudent income investors.

It is not a lot, but definitely something with which to nicely compound the portfolio or dedicate towards meaningful consumption coverage.

For instance, if we deploy $10,000 in 6% yield producing asset and redirect the collected cash flows back into the security and do so for 5 years straight, our portfolio (assuming constant pricing) would reach ~$13,500.

More importantly (at least for me), the annual paycheck would go up as well - from $600 to $830.

I will now elaborate on two blue-chip retirement income picks, which offer yields that are closer to the maximum range (or even slightly above that) for both de-risked and accelerated income compounding.

Pick #1 - Energy Transfer, 7.5%

Energy Transfer (ET) is a midstream player with a market cap of over $60 billion. The fact that it is a midstream partnership already speaks a lot about the underlying cash flows (and business risks). For ET as for most other MLPs, the cash flows are tied to long-term take-or-pay contracts, which introduce the following:

  • Volumetric downside risk floor.
  • Price (both nat gas and oil) downside risk floor.
  • Inflation-indexation in the form of embedded annual cash flow escalators.

In ET's case, ~90% of the EBITDA generation comes from these types of contracts.

What is quite unique about ET is that almost half of the current EBITDA is linked to natural gas projects, which are far less risky that pure-play oil exposures. Furthermore, if we dissect the CapEx plan for 2025, we will see that only 6% relates to traditional crude projects. The remaining chunk of ~$5 billion CapEx agenda is directed towards the inherently stable midstream and better natural gas plays. For example, it is exactly the natural gas fact, which is set to accommodate the expansion of data centers by providing stable base load energy (both directly and indirectly via gas-fired power plant).

This is a very great slide from ET's investor deck to consider in the aforementioned context:

The analyst consensus estimate on the future EPS growth indicates just that: i.e., a growing business.

Finally, what we have to also appreciate is that ET's pro-forma leverage is within the lower range of 4.0x to 4.5x, which is established as a target level for ET going forward. It is a very conservative level for such a stable cash-flow producing business. On top of that, if we annualize Q1, 2025 distributable cash flow generation and contextualize that with the Q1, 2025 distributions, then we will get a DCF payout that is less than 50%. In other words, ET has not only fortress balance sheet, but also a significant cash flow retention profile, which definitely comes in handy for fueling the CapEx growth in a financially prudent fashion (just as retirement income investors would want it to be).

Pick #2 - Main Street Capital, 6.6%

The other blue-chip retirement income behemoth is Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN), which is a business development company or a BDC. I know that some of you might instantly think here that MAIN has nothing to do with retirement portfolio due the fact that it makes money from small-cap business lending.

Conceptually, this kind of thinking is correct, but not in MAIN's case.

For example, the fact that MAIN has paid consistent and growing dividends since it tapped the IPO market in 2007 is a strong sign in itself. Think of it, MAIN has managed to survive the GFC, oil shock, COVID-19 and rapidly rising interest rate environment without having to make any sacrifices on the dividend policy front.

The reason behind MAIN's success is, in simple terms, conservative capital allocation strategy.

In my view, these are the most important aspects to consider;

  • MAIN has one of the lowest debt levels in the BDC sector (debt-to-equity of just 0.76x, while the sector average is 119%).
  • The base dividend coverage level is in the Top 3 rating (141% vs. sector average of 102%).
  • Both the net leverage and interest coverage of MAIN's portfolio businesses are clearly above what we see for the lion's share of BDCs.

Finally, as opposed to majority of peers, MAIN actively co-invests equity when underwriting loan investments to the high quality businesses. Since MAIN does not operate in the venture capital space, all of its businesses are nicely cash-flowing, which means that these equity injections produce (in most cases) dividend income. Yet, the bigger benefit is the optionality to capture equity-like upside from selling these stakes. In the current market environment, when the rates are heading lower, MAIN is very well-positioned. While the average BDC suffers from this through SOFR-driven yield-compression, MAIN can benefit by enjoying a more accommodative environment for equity sales.

In my humble opinion, MAIN is the right asset to include in the retirement income portfolio mix as an attractive yield-enhancer and diversifier.

Having said that, I want to make it clear that in terms of price and valuations, MAIN is not the best pick to choose. I have elaborated on this situation in more detail in my recent piece - click here -, but, frankly speaking, if the underlying investment objective is associated with just pure income extraction, then this argument is irrelevant.

The bottom line

The yields that are currently offered by strong blue-chip income-producers are still quite attractive. Even though the retirement income proof yield range has decreased from about 7% - 9% to 5% to 7%, it should be enough to move the needle for most income-oriented investors.

So, I do not think that sitting on sidelines makes sense, especially when we (as pure income investors) have such assets as ET and MAIN providing decent and well-protected yields.

data.title: Intrinsic Value Is Subjective: A Big Problem And A Big Opportunity For Investors
data.description:
Introduction: 10 Analysts, 10 Opinions on Value

Imagine gathering ten of the world's top analysts in one room. These are not amateurs. They have access to Bloomberg terminals, FactSet, satellite data, alternative datasets, AI-powered tools, management teams, industry consultants, and armies of junior analysts. They are MBAs, CFAs, CTAs - highly paid, highly resourced professionals in one of the most competitive industries on earth.

Now give them a single company to value. If the Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH) holds, their results should converge. After all, they have access to the best information and to the best analytical models to process it. Informational efficiency implies that when the best-informed and most rational minds go to work, they will arrive at the same - or at least very tightly clustered - conclusion.

But they won't. They will produce valuations that are not just different, but irreconcilably different. And this isn't a thought experiment. It is exactly what we observe in the real world.

This raises two big questions. First, the Efficient Market Hypothesis [EMH] claims that market are informationally efficient -- i.e. they reflect all available information and represent the best possible unbiased estimate of fundamental value. If the best experts in the world don't agree on what the right valuation is, by what means does the EMH claim that the market is able to sort out these disagreements and produce the "best unbiased estimate of fundamental value?" Second, if the most well-informed and most capable investors do agree on value, where does that leave the ordinary investor? How can an investor know which of the these many reasonable valuations, the market price will eventually reflect?

In this article we will take on both of these questions. First, we will show that the EMH's positive interpretation - the claim that markets converge on an informationally efficient price that reflects all available information - is empirically false. Then we will explore the implications for investors who must operate in a world where no such convergence ever occurs.

The Requirements for Informational Efficiency

Let's start with the EMH on its strongest footing. The positive interpretation of the EMH is the only interpretation that is actually consistent with the concept of efficiency and with the conditions that the EMH itself lays out as requirements for its existence.

For markets to converge on an informationally efficient price, four stringent conditions must hold:

  1. Symmetric information: All participants have equal access to the same data.

  2. Perfect rationality: All participants process information logically and without bias, and therefore arrive at the same conclusions.

  3. Frictionless communication: Insights are shared between market participants costlessly and instantly.

  4. Instantaneous dissemination: All information reaches all participants simultaneously and is instantaneously reflected in the market price.

No person with experience and/or knowledge of financial markets thinks that any of these conditions are met. In this regard, the EMH is dead in the water before the need of any serious analysis. However, it could be argued that if these conditions are met to a sufficient degree -- even if not perfectly -- then it could still be claimed that the market is "informationally efficient" in some important senses.

For example, if these conditions are met sufficiently (even if not perfectly), the EMH predicts that valuations should converge to a single informationally efficient price. This would occur, despite the fact that the requirements for informational efficiency are not met with respect to all market participants, as a result of the force that is exerted on market pries by the best-informed, most rational participants. EMH defenders claim that the actions of this special class of participants will dominate and ultimately push the market price towards its informationally efficient value. In other words, even if the average participant is biased or ill-informed, the smartest money overwhelms them, ensuring convergence of the market price at the informationally efficient value.

This is a testable claim. If it is true, then the valuations of highly resourced analysts - the very best in the industry - should converge tightly around a single number. If they don't, then the EMH fails because there is no informationally efficient valuation upon which analysts can converge on.

There is another interpretation of the EMH - a negative or "nihilist" version that argues informational efficiency doesn't mean convergence to the best unbiased estimate of fundamental value (as was originally claimed). Their argument is that "the price is always wrong," but that nobody can produce a price that is any less wrong. Said differently, the argument is that, "the price is not informationally efficient, but nobody's estimate of value is any more informationally efficient than the market price is." That defense we will take up in a separate article. Here, we focus on the positive version, which we believe is the only logically coherent expression of the EMH.

The Empirical Evidence Straight From The Market: The Magnificent Seven

Let's test the EMH inference of "convergence" in the real world. Consider the Magnificent Seven - Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), and NVIDIA (NVDA). These are the most scrutinized companies on earth, covered by dozens of the best analysts in the world at the best-resourced firms. If convergence is going to show up anywhere, it should be in analysts' valuations of these 7 stocks.

Yet their 12-month price targets span massive ranges.

Table 1: Analyst Price Target Dispersion (Magnificent 7, 2024)

Company Current Price Low Target Median Target High Target Low-to-High Dispersion (%) Median-to-High Dispersion (%)
AAPL $175 $150 $195 $240 60% 23%
MSFT $370 $300 $400 $480 60% 20%
AMZN $140 $105 $170 $230 119% 35%
GOOGL $130 $90 $150 $200 122% 33%
META $300 $220 $350 $450 105% 29%
TSLA $250 $85 $210 $400 371% 90%
NVDA $850 $400 $950 $1,400 250% 47%

Sources: Bloomberg, FactSet, Refinitiv (late 2024)

These are not estimates of retail traders firing from the hip. These are highly trained, highly-paid professionals operating in a hyper-competitive industry -- each with access to the best information available. And yet the dispersion is staggering: Tesla's 12-month targets range from $85 to $400 -- a difference of 371%. NVIDIA's 12-month targets from $400 to $1,400 -- a difference of 250%. And these figures vastly understate the differences in valuations among analysts because these are only 12-month targets. If you were to take a survey of the fundamental value of each stock the variations would be far more massive. To take just one example, the analyst team at ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK) has estimated Tesla's intrinsic value to be well over $2000, while other analysts estimate the intrinsic value of the company to be below $50.

If the EMH means anything at all, it must mean that under conditions approaching its own assumptions - rational, well-informed professionals with instant access to information and few frictions - valuations should converge tightly. They don't.

This is not an anomaly. It is the rule. Even in the most watched stocks in the world, disagreement is wide, persistent, and irreducible.

This poses a problem for the EMH and for active investors.

The Laboratory Tests: Vernon Smith's Shocking Findings

Defenders of the EMH might object: "Analysts disagree because they don't have exactly the same information. Some have better information, better channels, better models, or sharper judgment. If everyone truly had the same information and rationality, convergence would follow."

This response is like the child who tells the teacher, "the dog ate my homework." It is less a defense than an admission of defeat. It concedes that in the real world, the conditions for informational efficiency cannot exist.

But let's suspend disbelief and test their best-case defense. What if we created an environment where everyone really did have the same information and the analytical playing field was completely level? Would valuations converge tightly?

That is precisely the question economist Vernon Smith set out to answer in a series of laboratory experiments that eventually won him a Nobel Prize.
